### Title
Static OFAC Blocklist in Bitcoin Checker Canister Allows Newly Sanctioned Addresses to Bypass Compliance Checks — (File: rs/bitcoin/checker/lib/blocklist.rs)

### Summary
The Bitcoin checker canister stores the OFAC SDN (Specially Designated Nationals) list as a static compile-time constant array. When OFAC adds new sanctioned Bitcoin addresses to their list, the canister has no mechanism to reflect those changes until an NNS upgrade proposal is executed. During the gap between an OFAC list update and the next canister upgrade, newly sanctioned entities can deposit BTC and receive ckBTC (or withdraw ckBTC to their BTC address), bypassing the compliance check entirely.

### Finding Description
The `BTC_ADDRESS_BLOCKLIST` in `rs/bitcoin/checker/lib/blocklist.rs` is declared as a static `&[&str]` array compiled directly into the canister binary:

```rust
pub const BTC_ADDRESS_BLOCKLIST: &[&str] = &[
    "123WBUDmSJv4GctdVEz6Qq6z8nXSKrJ4KX",
    ...
];
```

The `is_blocked` function performs a binary search on this static array:

```rust
pub fn is_blocked(address: &Address) -> bool {
    BTC_ADDRESS_BLOCKLIST
        .binary_search(&address.to_string().as_ref())
        .is_ok()
}
```

The Bitcoin checker README explicitly acknowledges this design: *"The Bitcoin checker canister stores a copy of the SDN list internally. The list can only be modified by upgrading the Bitcoin checker canister itself, which requires an NNS proposal."*

The ckBTC minter calls `check_address` on the Bitcoin checker canister during BTC withdrawal requests (`retrieve_btc`). The `check_transaction` endpoint (used for deposits) also ultimately performs address lookups against the same static `BTC_ADDRESS_BLOCKLIST` — the HTTPS outcalls in `check_transaction` are only used to fetch transaction data from a block explorer, not to query an external SDN API.

The blocklist is regenerated from the OFAC SDN XML file using `rs/cross-chain/scripts/generate_blocklist.py`, which must be run manually and the result committed and deployed via NNS proposal. OFAC updates their SDN list multiple times per year.

This is structurally identical to the Convex adapter vulnerability: reward tokens (or in this case, sanctioned addresses) are captured at deployment time and not automatically tracked when the underlying external protocol state changes.

### Impact Explanation
A Bitcoin address that is added to the OFAC SDN list after the last Bitcoin checker canister upgrade will not appear in `BTC_ADDRESS_BLOCKLIST`. The `check_address` call from the ckBTC minter will return `Passed` for this address. As a result:

- **Deposits**: The ckBTC minter will mint ckBTC for a newly sanctioned entity's BTC deposit, because `check_transaction` resolves to the same static list.
- **Withdrawals**: The ckBTC minter will allow a newly sanctioned entity to call `retrieve_btc` and receive BTC at their sanctioned address, since `check_address` returns `Passed`.

This exposes the IC ecosystem to regulatory and compliance risk and allows sanctioned entities to use the ckBTC system during the update gap.

### Likelihood Explanation
OFAC updates the SDN list multiple times per year (additions and removals). Each update creates a window — potentially days or weeks — during which newly sanctioned BTC addresses are not blocked. Any entity whose address is added to the OFAC list between two Bitcoin checker upgrades can exploit this window. No privileged access is required; any user can call `retrieve_btc` or deposit BTC to the minter's address.

### Recommendation
1. Implement a dynamic blocklist stored in stable memory that can be updated via a dedicated NNS-gated update endpoint without requiring a full canister upgrade and redeployment.
2. Alternatively, implement a periodic HTTPS outcall to fetch the latest OFAC SDN list and update the in-memory blocklist automatically (subject to consensus on the outcall response).
3. At minimum, establish a monitoring process that triggers an NNS upgrade proposal promptly whenever OFAC publishes a new SDN list version.

### Proof of Concept
1. OFAC publishes an updated SDN list adding a new BTC address `
